annotations.label: Global maximum/minimum point

anchor.snippet: has a global (or absolute ) maximum point at

annotations.note: `IsMaxOn f s a` (and dually `IsMinOn`) formalizes a maximum point of `f` on a set; taking `s = univ` gives the global notion.

annotations.label: Local maximum/minimum point

anchor.snippet: then f is said to have a local (or relative ) maximum point

annotations.note: `IsLocalMax`/`IsLocalMin` are defined as `f x ≤ f a` (resp. `≥`) on a neighborhood of `a`.

annotations.label: Strict extremum

anchor.snippet: the concept of a strict extremum can be defined

annotations.note: Grep finds no `IsStrictMaxOn`/`IsStrictLocalMax` analogue with the strict-inequality variant in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strict global maximum iff unique

anchor.snippet: a point is a strict global maximum point if and only if it is the unique global maximum point

annotations.note: No strict-extremum predicate is defined, so this characterization is absent.

annotations.label: Extrema on compact domain

anchor.snippet: A continuous real-valued function with a compact domain always has a maximum point and a minimum point

annotations.note: `IsCompact.exists_isMaxOn` and `IsCompact.exists_isMinOn` give a max/min point of a continuous function on a nonempty compact set.

annotations.label: Extreme value theorem

anchor.snippet: If a function is continuous on a closed interval, then by the extreme value theorem

annotations.note: The extreme value theorem is stated as `IsCompact.exists_isMinOn`/`exists_isMaxOn`, generalizing closed intervals to arbitrary compact sets.

annotations.label: Location of global extrema

anchor.snippet: a global maximum (or minimum) either must be a local maximum

annotations.note: `IsMaxOn.isLocalMax` (when the set is a neighborhood) captures part of this, but the boundary-vs-interior trichotomy is not stated as a single proposition.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem on critical points

anchor.snippet: Fermat's theorem states that local extrema in the interior of a domain must occur at critical points

annotations.note: Fermat's theorem is `IsLocalMin.deriv_eq_zero`, `IsLocalMax.deriv_eq_zero`, and `IsLocalExtr.deriv_eq_zero`.

annotations.label: Maximum of piecewise function

anchor.snippet: For any function that is defined piecewise , one finds a maximum

annotations.note: No general lemma about taking the max of a piecewise-defined function over its pieces is present.

annotations.label: Rectangular enclosure fencing

anchor.snippet: assume a situation where someone has

annotations.note: This is a worked example (maximize area subject to perimeter constraint) and is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Necessary conditions for local maximum (multivariable)

anchor.section: Functions of more than one variable
anchor.snippet: the necessary conditions for a local maximum are similar to those of a function with only one variable

annotations.note: `IsLocalExtr.fderiv_eq_zero` (and `hasFDerivAt_eq_zero`) gives vanishing of the Fréchet derivative at a local extremum, the multivariable necessary condition.

annotations.label: Single critical point implies global minimum (1D)

anchor.section: Functions of more than one variable
anchor.snippet: if a bounded differentiable function f defined on a closed interval in the real line has a single critical point

annotations.note: I find no statement equating a unique interior critical point of a bounded differentiable function on a closed interval with a global extremum.

annotations.label: Local but not global minimum in 2D

anchor.section: Functions of more than one variable
anchor.snippet: whose only critical point is at (0,0), which is a local minimum

annotations.note: This specific counterexample (e.g. Peano's surface) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Extremum of a functional via calculus of variations

anchor.section: Maxima or minima of a functional
anchor.snippet: then the extremum is found using the calculus of variations

annotations.note: Calculus of variations / Euler-Lagrange machinery is absent from Mathlib (grep finds nothing).

annotations.label: Maximal element of a set

anchor.snippet: if an ordered set S has a greatest element m , then m is a maximal element of the set

annotations.note: `Maximal P x` (and the dual `Minimal`) define maximal/minimal elements satisfying a predicate.

annotations.label: Least upper bound in a superset

anchor.snippet: then m is a least upper bound of S in T

annotations.note: `IsLUB s a` is defined as `IsLeast (upperBounds s) a`, matching the least-upper-bound definition.

annotations.label: Least vs minimal element in a partial order

anchor.snippet: a least element (i.e., one that is less than all others) should not be confused with the minimal element

annotations.note: `IsLeast` (least element) and `Minimal` (minimal element) are both defined and distinguished in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Greatest and maximal element of a poset

anchor.snippet: a greatest element of a partially ordered set (poset) is an upper bound of the set which is contained within the set

annotations.note: `IsGreatest s a := a ∈ s ∧ a ∈ upperBounds s` matches the definition; `Maximal` covers the maximal-element notion.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of least/greatest in a poset

anchor.snippet: Any least element or greatest element of a poset is unique

annotations.note: `IsLeast.unique` proves two least elements of a set are equal; the dual `IsGreatest.unique` exists adjacently.

annotations.label: Extrema in a totally ordered set

anchor.snippet: In a totally ordered set, or chain , all elements are mutually comparable

annotations.note: Total/linear orders are pervasive (`LinearOrder`, `IsTotal`) and many lemmas about extrema specialize to them, but the article statement isn't itself a single theorem.

annotations.label: Finite chains have max and min

anchor.snippet: If a chain is finite, then it will always have a maximum and a minimum

annotations.note: `Set.Finite.exists_max_image` (and `Finset.exists_max_image`) yield a max element of a nonempty finite set in a linear order.

annotations.label: Natural numbers have no maximum

anchor.snippet: the set of natural numbers has no maximum, though it has a minimum

annotations.note: `Nat` has a `NoMaxOrder` instance, so `not_bddAbove_univ` shows `(univ : Set ℕ)` has no greatest element while `0` is its least.

annotations.label: Argument of the maximum (argmax/argmin)

anchor.snippet: the arguments of the maxima (abbreviated arg max or argmax) and arguments of the minima

annotations.note: `Function.argmin`/`argminOn` (over well-founded orders) and `List.argmax`/`argmin` formalize the argmax/argmin operators.
